title: A Selection from the Discourses of Epictetus with the Encheiridion
description: A Selection from the Discourses of Epictetus with the Encheiridion — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: Stoic philosophy from the lectures of Epictetus, as recorded by Arrian and translated by George Long

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: "Abstinence"
memes.description: "The Stoic practice of voluntary self-denial — abstinence (enkrateia) is treated by Epictetus as an exercise in moral freedom, a demonstration that the body serves the will rather than the reverse, and one of the powers available for turning every circumstance to use."

memes.label: "Achilles"
memes.description: "Homeric hero whom Epictetus uses twice as a moral exemplum — of misapplied preconceptions in the quarrel with Agamemnon, and of ruin brought on by anger rather than external loss."

memes.label: "Action"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, action (hormē; praxis) is governed by the discipline of impulse — right action flows from correct judgments, and each action either preserves or destroys the moral character of the agent."

memes.label: "Agamemnon"
memes.description: "Homeric king of the Greek host at Troy, whom Epictetus uses as an exemplum of misapplied preconceptions and of the powerful man who is nonetheless wretched."

memes.label: "Alexander of Troy"
memes.description: "The Trojan prince (Paris) whom Epictetus, always naming him 'Alexander,' uses as the type of the man ruined not by war but by the false opinion that carried off another's wife."

memes.label: "Alexander the Great"
memes.description: "The Macedonian king whom Epictetus cites twice — as the worldly power Diogenes addressed without fear, and as the man whose grief drove him to burn the gods' temples."

memes.label: Anger
memes.description: A destructive passion arising from the judgment of being wronged; Stoic analysis and therapeutic approach to anger.

memes.label: "Animal"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's anthropology, the concept of animal serves to define the unique nature of man — the rational animal (zōon logikon) who shares body with beasts but reason with gods, and whose proper end is not mere use of appearances but the understanding and interpretation of divine works."

memes.label: "Anxiety"
memes.description: "The Stoic diagnosis and therapy of anxiety (solicitudo) — how valuing externals creates perturbation, and how the discipline of desire eliminates it."

memes.label: "Anytus"
memes.description: "One of the Athenian accusers of Socrates, cited by Epictetus — in the Discourses and in the closing words of the Encheiridion — as the enemy who could kill Socrates but not harm him."

memes.label: "Appearances"
memes.description: "The impressions (phantasiai) that present themselves to the mind — the raw material of Stoic ethics, which must be tested by reason before assent is given."

memes.label: "Arrogance"
memes.description: "Arrogance (huperēphania) in Stoic ethics is the opinion that one lacks nothing and is above correction — a vice that Epictetus identifies as one of the two great obstacles to moral progress, alongside distrust."

memes.label: "Assent"
memes.description: "The act by which the rational faculty accepts or rejects an impression (phantasia) as true — the locus of moral responsibility in Stoic epistemology."

memes.label: "Athens"
memes.description: "The philosophical capital of Greece, invoked in the Discourses as an object of homesick longing to be overcome and as the city that condemned Socrates."

memes.label: "Athlete"
memes.description: "The athletic metaphor in Epictetus's moral philosophy — the Stoic practitioner as an athlete training in the contest of life, developing endurance and skill through repeated exercise."

memes.label: "Attention (Prosoche)"
memes.description: "The Stoic spiritual practice of continuous mindful attention to one's judgments, desires, and actions — the essential discipline for maintaining moral progress."

memes.label: "Aversion"
memes.description: "In Stoic ethics, aversion (ekklisis) is the impulse to avoid what is judged bad — paired with desire (orexis) as one of the two fundamental movements of the will, and the subject of Epictetus's first of the Three Disciplines."

memes.label: "Banquet"
memes.description: "The banquet (symposion) as a metaphor for life in Stoic ethics — from the Encheiridion's instruction to behave at life as at a banquet, partaking with detachment and grace."

memes.label: Blame
memes.description: The Stoic ethical principle that blaming others reflects an undisciplined judgment; the philosopher blames no one, neither gods nor fellow humans, because all error results from ignorance of the good.

memes.label: Body
memes.description: The physical body as an external and indifferent in Stoic ethics; not within the ruling faculty's control and therefore not a true good or evil.

memes.label: "Books"
memes.description: "Books in Epictetan philosophy are instruments of philosophical training — useful preparation for the art of living, but dangerous when they become a substitute for practice."

memes.label: "Caution"
memes.description: "The rational circumspection regarding matters of moral choice — Epictetus teaches that caution and confidence must be properly allocated, with caution directed at the will and confidence directed at externals."

memes.label: "Change"
memes.description: "In Stoic physics and ethics, change (metabolē) is the universal condition of the cosmos — all externals are in constant flux — and the Stoic sage cultivates unchangeable firmness of mind in response to this fundamental truth."

memes.label: Character
memes.description: Moral character (ēthos/prosōpon) as the foundation of Stoic ethics; the quality that defines a person across different social roles and is shaped by every action.

memes.label: "Child"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, the child serves as a key metaphor for the untrained mind — one who mistakes appearances for reality, lacks knowledge, and reacts to externals without the discipline of philosophical reason."

memes.label: "Chrysippus"
memes.description: "The third head of the Stoic school (c. 279–206 BCE) — the great systematizer of Stoic doctrine, cited by Epictetus as a philosophical authority."

memes.label: Citizen
memes.description: In Stoic cosmopolitanism, every human being is a citizen of the cosmos (kosmopolitēs), owing primary allegiance to the divine city governed by universal law, not to any particular earthly state.

memes.label: "Confidence"
memes.description: "The boldness with which a Stoic faces things outside the will — paired paradoxically with caution toward things within the will, where the true good and evil lie."

memes.label: "Constancy"
memes.description: "The Stoic virtue of steadfastness and firmness in maintaining right judgments and moral character regardless of external pressures, threats, or temptations."

memes.label: "Contentment"
memes.description: "The Stoic disposition of satisfied acceptance with what one has, arising from limiting desire to what is within one's power and recognizing the divine order in all that happens."

memes.label: Contents
memes.description: Table of contents for A Selection from the Discourses of Epictetus with the Encheiridion

memes.label: "Contest"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, the athletic contest (agōn) serves as a central metaphor for moral struggle — the philosopher is an athlete of the soul, and the contest is waged not against others but against one's own passions and false judgments."

memes.label: "Courage"
memes.description: "The Stoic virtue of facing danger, difficulty, and death with steadfastness — courage (andreia) is the knowledge of what is and is not truly terrible."

memes.label: "Crito"
memes.description: "Athenian friend of Socrates whom Epictetus names twice as the addressee of Socrates's formula of submission to the will of the gods."

memes.label: "Croesus"
memes.description: "Proverbially wealthy last king of Lydia whom Epictetus cites twice — as the unreachable summit of riches, and as proof that possessions do not contain happiness."

memes.label: Custom
memes.description: In Stoic ethics, custom (ethos) refers to habitual patterns of thought and action; Epictetus emphasizes that philosophical training consists in replacing unreflective custom with deliberate habituation to right judgment.

memes.label: "Cynicism"
memes.description: "The Greek philosophical school founded by Diogenes, characterized by asceticism and rejection of convention, which Epictetus presents as a demanding divine vocation rather than a lifestyle choice."

memes.label: "Danger"
memes.description: "In Stoic ethics, true danger is moral corruption alone; physical dangers are indifferents that cannot harm the ruling faculty."

memes.label: "Death"
memes.description: "The end of bodily life — for the Stoic, the ultimate test case of the doctrine of indifferents and the subject of Epictetus's most pointed ethical training."

memes.label: "Desire"
memes.description: "The first of Epictetus's three disciplines — the impulse toward a perceived good, which must be redirected entirely toward what is within our power."

memes.label: "Despair"
memes.description: "In Stoic philosophy, despair (apognōsis) is a form of madness or radical misjudgment in which one abandons the pursuit of virtue, often contrasted with the steady confidence of the philosopher."

memes.label: "Diogenes of Sinope"
memes.description: "Cynic philosopher (c. 412–323 BCE) — Epictetus's second great exemplar after Socrates, representing radical independence from externals."

memes.label: "Three Disciplines"
memes.description: "Epictetus's tripartite program of moral training: the discipline of desire, the discipline of action, and the discipline of assent."

memes.label: "Discourse 50: THAT WE OUGHT TO PROCEED WITH CIRCUMSPECTION TO EVERYTHING.[Footnote: Compare Encheiridion, 29.]—In every act consider what precedes and what follows, and then proceed to the act. If you do not consider, you will at first begin with spirit, since you have not thought at all of the things which follow; but afterwards when some consequences have shown themselves, you will basely desist (from that which you have begun).—I wish to conquer at the Olympic games"
memes.description: "THAT WE OUGHT TO PROCEED WITH CIRCUMSPECTION TO EVERYTHING.[Footnote: Compare Encheiridion, 29.]—In every act consider what precedes and what follows, and then proceed to the act. If you do not consider, you will at first begin with spirit, since you have not thought at all of the things which follow; but afterwards when some consequences have shown themselves, you will basely desist (from that which you have begun).—I wish to conquer at the Olympic games — from A Selection from the Discourses of Epictetus"

memes.label: "Disease"
memes.description: "In Stoicism, the passions are likened to diseases of the soul (nosēmata) that grow progressively and require philosophical therapy to cure."

memes.label: "Disputation"
memes.description: "The Stoic art of logical discussion (dialektikē) — Epictetus teaches that knowing the rules of disputation is useless without the proper disposition of patience, gentleness, and respect for one's interlocutor."

memes.label: "Distrust"
memes.description: "In Epictetus, either the vicious opinion that one cannot be happy under one's circumstances, or the virtuous suspicion of mere appearances and externals that marks the beginning of philosophy."

memes.label: "Divination"
memes.description: "The practice of seeking knowledge of future events through supernatural signs — criticized by Epictetus as driven by cowardice and unnecessary for one who understands the nature of good and evil."

memes.label: "Divinity"
memes.description: "In the Discourses, divinity (theion) denotes the rational, providential, and moral character of the cosmic order — the divine administration that the wise person freely follows. Epictetus uses the concept to ground ethics in theology: because the divine is faithful, benevolent, and rational, the human being who would be good must become the same."

memes.label: "Doctrine"
memes.description: "A settled philosophical conviction or principle that governs action, distinguished from mere intellectual opinion in Epictetan Stoicism."

memes.label: "Duties"
memes.description: "Appropriate acts (kathēkonta) measured by relations — the Stoic doctrine that our obligations arise from the natural roles we occupy as father, son, citizen, and human being."

memes.label: "Education"
memes.description: "For Epictetus, education (paideia) is not the acquisition of book-learning or rhetorical skill but the learning how to adapt one's natural preconceptions to particular circumstances in conformity with nature — a life-long practical training in virtue rather than an intellectual accomplishment."

memes.label: "Egypt"
memes.description: "A nation Epictetus repeatedly names among Jews, Syrians and Romans to show that shared moral preconceptions are disputed only in their application, and that custom alone cannot settle what is right."

memes.label: "Encheiridion"
memes.description: "The Encheiridion (Manual or Handbook) is Arrian's concise compilation of Epictetus's core teachings, comprising 52 sections that distill the essential Stoic doctrines from the longer Discourses."

memes.label: "Endurance"
memes.description: "The Stoic power to bear hardship, pain, and difficulty without complaint — endurance (karteria) is a faculty given by nature, to be exercised for oneself, not for display."

memes.label: Envy
memes.description: A destructive passion arising from false judgments about externals; Stoic analysis and remedy for envy.

memes.label: "Epictetus"
memes.description: "Greek Stoic philosopher (c. 50–135 CE), formerly enslaved, whose teachings were recorded by his student Arrian in the Discourses and Encheiridion."

memes.label: Epictetus (Biographical Note)
memes.description: A biographical note on Epictetus by George Long, translator

memes.label: "Epicurus"
memes.description: "Greek philosopher (341–270 BCE), founder of the Epicurean school, whom Epictetus treats as the great rival to Stoic ethics and whose doctrine of pleasure he repeatedly refutes."

memes.label: "Error"
memes.description: "In Stoic philosophy, moral error (hamartēma) stems from faulty judgment and ignorance, distinct from intentional vice."

memes.label: "Eteocles"
memes.description: "Son of Oedipus and king of Thebes whose fatal quarrel with his brother Polynices Epictetus cites to show that it is opinion, not circumstance, that makes men enemies."

memes.label: "Euphrates"
memes.description: "Stoic philosopher of Epictetus's own era, renowned for eloquence, whom Epictetus names as the type of the impressive speaker people rashly wish to imitate."

memes.label: "Evil"
memes.description: "In Stoic philosophy, evil (kakon) is located exclusively in the moral sphere — the corruption of the ruling principle through false judgments, not in external events like death, pain, or poverty."

memes.label: "Example"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's teaching, examples (paradeigmata) — the living models of Socrates, Diogenes, and other virtuous figures — are central to moral education: they demonstrate that the Stoic ideal is attainable, provide concrete patterns for imitation, and shame the student out of complacency by showing what a human being can become."

memes.label: "Exercise"
memes.description: "Spiritual exercise (askēsis) in Epictetus is the practical training of the ruling faculty — daily discipline in desire, aversion, and assent — by which one progresses from knowing Stoic principles to living them, transforming every external circumstance into material for virtue."

memes.label: "Exile"
memes.description: "Banishment from one's native city — treated by Epictetus as a classic Stoic 'indifferent,' an external event that cannot harm the moral character of a wise person."

memes.label: "Externals"
memes.description: "Things outside our control (ta ouk eph hemin) — health, wealth, reputation, and all that is neither good nor bad in Stoic ethics."

memes.label: "Faith"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, faith (pistis) denotes fidelity to the divine order, moral constancy, and the trustworthy character that results from aligning one's will with nature — a virtue manifested in action rather than mere belief."

memes.label: "Fate"
memes.description: "Fate (heimarmenē) in Stoic philosophy is the rational and providential ordering of all events — Epictetus teaches that the wise person accepts fate not with passive resignation but with active, willing consent, aligning their own will with the divine order of the cosmos."

memes.label: "Fear"
memes.description: "The Stoic analysis of fear as a passion arising from false judgments about external things, to be overcome by correctly distinguishing what is and is not within one's power."

memes.label: "Festival"
memes.description: "The Stoic metaphor of life as a festival (panēgyris) — Epictetus uses the image of a public celebration to teach acceptance of one's appointed time, gratitude for participation, and the proper attitude toward departure."

memes.label: "Fever"
memes.description: "Fever (pyretos) as a paradigmatic test of Stoic character in Epictetus — a bodily indifferent that reveals whether one's principles are genuine or mere words."

memes.label: "Fidelity"
memes.description: "The Stoic virtue of faithfulness and trustworthiness — fidelity (pistis) is a core social virtue that Epictetus treats as an essential quality of the philosopher, paired with modesty and constancy as the marks of a person living according to nature."

memes.label: "Firmness"
memes.description: "Firmness (ametaptōsia) is the Stoic virtue of unchangeable stability of mind — a state of rational constancy in which one's judgments about what is good and evil are so firmly grounded that no external event can disturb one's inner tranquillity."

memes.label: "Flattery"
memes.description: "The Stoic moral vice (kolakeia) of winning favour through insincere praise — trading one's own judgment and freedom for another's approval."

memes.label: "Fortune"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, fortune (tychē) is classified among external things independent of the will — neither good nor evil in itself, but the raw material for virtue when met with correct judgment. True good fortune is not favorable circumstance but the tranquility of a mind aligned with nature."

memes.label: "Freedom"
memes.description: "The Stoic ideal of inner freedom — the power to live according to one's rational will, unenslaved by externals or passions."

memes.label: "Friendship"
memes.description: "The Stoic understanding of true friendship as possible only among the wise, grounded in shared commitment to virtue and the correct placement of one's good in the will."

memes.label: "Gladiator"
memes.description: "The gladiator (monomachos) as a metaphor for the Stoic engaged in moral combat — a stage in Epictetus's progression from wrestler to gladiator to philosopher."

memes.label: "The Good"
memes.description: "In Stoic ethics, the sole good is virtue — all externals are classified as indifferents (adiaphora), neither good nor bad in themselves."

memes.label: "Grief"
memes.description: "Grief (lupē) is one of the four primary Stoic passions — a distress of the soul arising from the judgment that something present is evil. Epictetus treats grief not as a natural process to be endured but as a cognitive error to be corrected: grief arises from valuing externals, and it dissolves when the judgment is retrained."

memes.label: "Habit"
memes.description: "The repeated practice that shapes moral character — Epictetus teaches that every faculty is strengthened by its corresponding actions, and that philosophy is the training of right habits."

memes.label: "Happiness"
memes.description: "The natural aim of human life (eudaimonia) — in Stoic thought, achieved solely through virtue, not through external goods or pleasures."

memes.label: Health
memes.description: Bodily health as a preferred indifferent in Stoic ethics; naturally worth pursuing but neither a genuine good nor essential to happiness.

memes.label: Ruling Principle (Hēgemonikon)
memes.description: The commanding faculty of the mind that judges impressions and directs action in Stoic psychology.

memes.label: Honor
memes.description: Honor (timē) as an external indifferent in Stoic ethics; public recognition, rank, and status are neither good nor evil in themselves — virtue alone confers true honor.

memes.label: "Hope"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, hope (elpis) is an impulse toward a perceived future good — a 'profession' of desire that must be disciplined by the recognition that only virtue and the proper use of impressions are truly worth hoping for."

memes.label: "Ignorance"
memes.description: "Stoic intellectualism — the Socratic doctrine that all vice and error stem from ignorance (agnoia), and that knowledge of the good is sufficient for virtuous action."

memes.label: "Impression"
memes.description: "The appearance or presentation (phantasia) to the mind — the raw material of knowledge that must be examined before assent is given."

memes.label: "Impulse"
memes.description: "Impulse (hormē) is a technical Stoic term for the soul's movement toward or away from an object — the second of Epictetus's Three Topics (Topics of Ethics), governing the realm of appropriate action and the proper direction of desire and aversion."

memes.label: Indifferents (Adiaphora)
memes.description: The Stoic classification of things that are neither intrinsically good nor evil; only virtue and vice have moral value.

memes.label: "Insult"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, an insult is an external event whose power to harm resides entirely in the judgment of the recipient — bearing insults with equanimity is a core exercise in the discipline of assent and a demonstration of moral freedom."

memes.label: "Jealousy"
memes.description: "Jealousy (phthonos) is a Stoic passion of distress at another's success or possessions — Epictetus identifies it as one of the emotional disturbances that arise from wrongly valuing externals and that dissolve when one places the good solely within the sphere of choice."

memes.label: "Judgment"
memes.description: "The act of evaluating impressions and forming beliefs — the cognitive mechanism through which virtue or vice is realized in Stoic psychology."

memes.label: "Justice"
memes.description: "The Stoic cardinal virtue of giving each person their due — rooted not in external law but in the recognition of shared rational nature and the proper use of the will."

memes.label: "Kinship"
memes.description: "In Stoic philosophy, kinship (syngeneia) denotes the natural affinity between humans and the divine through shared reason, contrasting with the base kinship of the flesh."

memes.label: "Knowledge"
memes.description: "In Stoic epistemology as treated by Epictetus, knowledge (epistēmē) is the secure and unshakeable cognition achieved through philosophical training — distinct from mere opinion, and the foundation of the virtuous life."

memes.label: "Lacedaemon"
memes.description: "Ancient Sparta, whose citizens' disciplined endurance Epictetus cites as proof that pain is bearable once reason judges it so."

memes.label: Law
memes.description: In Stoic philosophy, law (nomos) is the rational principle governing the cosmos; human laws are mere conventions, while the divine law of nature is universal, immutable, and the foundation of all ethical obligation.

memes.label: "Learning"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, learning (mathēsis; paideia) is not the accumulation of doctrines but the practical discipline of adapting one's natural preconceptions to particular circumstances — education as the formation of character."

memes.label: "Lie"
memes.description: "In Stoic philosophy, lying is a corruption of the rational faculty — the knowing assent to falsehood — and therefore a moral evil that damages the liar's own ruling principle before it harms anyone else."

memes.label: "Logic"
memes.description: "One of the three fields of Stoic philosophy, concerned with sound reasoning, the testing of arguments, and the avoidance of error in judgment."

memes.label: "Love"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, love (philia; erōs) is treated as a faculty of the rational mind that must be governed by correct judgments about what is truly good, distinguishing proper affection from passionate attachment to externals."

memes.label: "Madness"
memes.description: "The Stoic understanding of madness (mania) as a state of cognitive derangement in which reason is abandoned, treated by Epictetus as the antithesis of philosophical freedom."

memes.label: "Magnanimity"
memes.description: "Greatness of soul (megalopsychia) — the Stoic virtue of rising above fortune's blows through the possession of an indomitable rational faculty."

memes.label: "Mask"
memes.description: "The tragic mask metaphor in Epictetus — adapted from Socrates, the image teaches that death, pain, exile, and all apparent evils are like theatrical masks: frightening only to those who do not examine them, hollow and harmless when turned over and inspected."

memes.label: "Measure"
memes.description: "The Stoic concept of proper measurement (metron) — the rational standard by which actions, duties, and judgments are evaluated against nature rather than mere appearance."

memes.label: "Medea"
memes.description: "Tragic heroine whom Epictetus makes the paradigm of the deceived will — one who murders her own children because passion has convinced her it is the profitable course."

memes.label: "Menelaus"
memes.description: "Homeric king of Sparta whom Epictetus cites twice — as proof that the Trojan War sprang from mere appearances, and as an example of a guest-friendship dissolved by a disputed external."

memes.label: "Moderation"
memes.description: "The Stoic quality of self-restraint and gentleness in conduct — moderation (to metrion) is the outward expression of an inner order, closely related to temperance and attention, and easily lost through carelessness."

memes.label: "Modesty"
memes.description: "The Stoic virtue of self-respect and a sense of shame before what is base — modesty (aidōs) is treated by Epictetus as a natural human endowment, paired with fidelity as the twin foundation of social life and the protection of character."

memes.label: "Musonius Rufus"
memes.description: "Roman Stoic philosopher (c. 30–100 CE) whose lectures Epictetus attended as a young slave; the immediate teacher in whose school Epictetus was formed."

memes.label: "Nature"
memes.description: "The rational order of the cosmos — the standard against which all actions and judgments are measured in Stoic ethics, identified with God, reason, and fate."

memes.label: "Necessity"
memes.description: "In Stoic doctrine, necessity (anankē) is the causal determinism that governs the cosmos — the unbreakable chain of cause and effect that is identical with Fate and divine Reason. For Epictetus, the recognition of necessity is not fatalism but liberation: by distinguishing what is necessary from what is in our power, we learn to redirect desire from the impossible to the possible."

memes.label: "Nero"
memes.description: "Roman emperor (r. 54–68 CE) whom Epictetus invokes as the type of the tyrant and the debased character — the 'stamp' a good man must reject."

memes.label: "Nicopolis"
memes.description: "City in northwestern Greece where Epictetus established his philosophical school after being banished from Rome in 93 CE."

memes.label: "Obedience"
memes.description: "The Stoic virtue of willing submission to divine will, natural law, and rational authority — obedience in Epictetus is not servility but the free alignment of one's own will with the order of nature, expressed in willing compliance with what God and reason command."

memes.label: "Olympia"
memes.description: "Sanctuary of Zeus in Elis, site of the Olympic Games — used by Epictetus as a metaphor for the discipline required in the philosophical life."

memes.label: "Opinion"
memes.description: "The judgment or belief (δόγμα, dogma) that determines a person's emotional responses — the Stoic teaching that it is not events but our opinions about them that disturb us."

memes.label: Pain
memes.description: Physical pain (ponos) as a classic Stoic indifferent; not an evil in itself but an impression whose power depends on judgment.

memes.label: "Passion"
memes.description: "In Stoic psychology, an irrational and excessive impulse (pathos) — a disturbance of the soul arising from false judgments about what is good or bad."

memes.label: Patience
memes.description: The Stoic virtue of enduring difficulties and provocations with calm rationality, rooted in correct judgment about what is truly good.

memes.label: "Perception"
memes.description: "In Stoic philosophy, perception (aisthēsis) is the awareness of one's own moral condition and of disagreement among men — the starting point of philosophy according to Epictetus."

memes.label: "Philosophy"
memes.description: "Epictetus's conception of philosophy as the art of living — a practical discipline of self-examination and moral training, not theoretical speculation."

memes.label: "Physician"
memes.description: "In Stoic philosophy, the physician (iatros) serves as a key metaphor for the philosopher as healer of the soul, applying rational therapy to the passions."

memes.label: "Piety"
memes.description: "The Stoic virtue of right opinion and conduct toward the gods — piety (eusebeia) consists in holding correct beliefs about divine providence, desiring in accordance with the cosmic order, and performing traditional observances without superstition."

memes.label: Pity
memes.description: A passion arising from the mistaken judgment that external misfortune is a genuine evil; the Stoic critique of pity.

memes.label: "Plato"
memes.description: "Athenian philosopher (c. 428–348 BCE) whom Epictetus cites as an authority on the will's relation to truth and on the philosopher's preparation for suffering."

memes.label: Pleasure
memes.description: Bodily and mental pleasure as a passion and an indifferent in Stoic ethics; to be distrusted rather than pursued, since it can enslave the ruling faculty.

memes.label: "Polynices"
memes.description: "Exiled son of Oedipus who warred on Thebes to reclaim the throne; Epictetus pairs him with his brother Eteocles to show that false opinion, not circumstance, makes men enemies."

memes.label: "Possession"
memes.description: "Possessions (ktēsis) as external things in Stoic ethics — classified among indifferents (adiaphora) and incapable of contributing to genuine happiness or virtue."

memes.label: Poverty
memes.description: An external condition that is neither good nor evil in Stoic ethics; the proper Stoic attitude toward material lack.

memes.label: "Power"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Discourses, power (dynamis) is radically redefined: true power belongs not to the tyrant who controls bodies but to the philosopher who controls judgment. The central distinction between what is 'in our power' (eph' hēmin) and what is not is the foundation of the entire Stoic ethical system as Epictetus presents it."

memes.label: Praise
memes.description: In Stoic ethics, praise (epainos) from the multitude is an external indifferent; the philosopher seeks only the praise that comes from right judgment, not from the applause of the crowd.

memes.label: "Precept"
memes.description: "A practical rule or theorem (theōrēma) derived from philosophical doctrine for guiding conduct in specific situations — the application layer of Stoic ethics"

memes.label: "Preconceptions"
memes.description: "Innate notions (prolēpseis) common to all humans, forming the natural foundation for philosophical reasoning about good, evil, justice, and beauty."

memes.label: "Preparation"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, preparation (paraskeuē) is the disciplined readiness cultivated through philosophical training — the capacity to meet any external event with equanimity, having rehearsed one's judgments in advance."

memes.label: "Progress"
memes.description: "The Stoic concept of moral advancement (prokope) toward virtue, measured not by learning but by the gradual transformation of one's desires, aversions, and impulses."

memes.label: "Prohairesis"
memes.description: "The faculty of moral choice or purpose — the core of Epictetus's psychology and the seat of human freedom and responsibility."

memes.label: "Promise"
memes.description: "The Stoic concept of fulfilling the distinctive promise (epangelia) of human nature — to live according to reason rather than descending to the level of beasts."

memes.label: "Providence"
memes.description: "The Stoic doctrine that the cosmos is ordered by divine reason for the benefit of all."

memes.label: "Purity"
memes.description: "The Stoic ideal of a soul cleansed of false opinions — purity is not ritual cleanliness but the possession of correct judgments about good and evil."

memes.label: "Reason"
memes.description: "The rational faculty (logos) — the defining power of human nature and the seat of moral choice in Stoic philosophy."

memes.label: Reputation
memes.description: Social reputation as a classic Stoic indifferent; Epictetus calls it "the noise of madmen" — neither a good nor an evil, and not worth the soul's attention.

memes.label: "Rhetoric"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Discourses, rhetoric (rhētorikē) represents the archetype of false education — the cultivation of elegant speech and external adornment without corresponding moral substance, contrasted sharply with philosophy which trains the will rather than the tongue."

memes.label: Roles
memes.description: The Stoic doctrine (prosōpa/personae) that each person holds multiple social roles — human, citizen, son, brother, and more — each carrying a distinct character and its own proper duties.

memes.label: "Rome"
memes.description: "Capital of the Roman Empire — where Epictetus lived as a slave and later taught philosophy before the philosopher ban of 93 CE."

memes.label: "Sacrifice"
memes.description: "In Stoic philosophy, sacrifice (thysia) is transformed from external ritual into an act of inner piety — the offering of correct judgments and right use of impressions to God."

memes.label: "Sage"
memes.description: "The Stoic ideal of the perfectly wise person (sophos) — one who has achieved complete virtue and freedom from passion."

memes.label: School
memes.description: The philosophical school (scholē) in Epictetus's teaching is both a physical place of instruction and a metaphor for the training ground where the soul is prepared for the contests of life.

memes.label: Security
memes.description: The inner stability that comes from placing one's good in what is within one's power; a characteristic fruit of Stoic practice.

memes.label: "Self-Conceit"
memes.description: "Self-conceit (oiēsis) in Stoic ethics is the false belief that one already knows what one does not know — the primary obstacle to philosophical progress and learning."

memes.label: "Self-Discipline"
memes.description: "Self-discipline (askēsis) as core Stoic practice — the systematic training of the rational faculty to judge appearances correctly and maintain the will in accordance with nature."

memes.label: Shame
memes.description: The Stoic analysis of shame (aischyne) as distinct from healthy modesty (aidos); a passion arising from concern with others' opinions.

memes.label: "Slave"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, slavery is primarily an inner condition of moral bondage to externals, not merely a legal status — and he speaks from personal experience as a former slave in Rome."

memes.label: "Sleep"
memes.description: "Epictetus's use of sleep as a metaphor for spiritual inattention and a practical test of the Stoic discipline of assent, including the famous Pythagorean evening examination."

memes.label: "Socrates"
memes.description: "Athenian philosopher (c. 470–399 BCE) — the primary ethical model in Epictetus's Discourses, cited more than any other historical figure."

memes.label: "Solitude"
memes.description: "The Stoic condition of self-sufficiency — to be alone yet not helpless, able to be one's own companion through the possession of right reason and inner resources."

memes.label: "Sophist"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's teaching, the sophist is the antithesis of the true philosopher — one who deploys clever arguments for display and verbal victory rather than for moral improvement and the discovery of truth."

memes.label: "Sorrow"
memes.description: "The Stoic passion of distress (lupē), an emotional perturbation that Epictetus treats as incompatible with freedom and the life of reason."

memes.label: "Soul"
memes.description: "In Stoic psychology, the soul (psyche) is the corporeal breath that animates the body and houses the ruling principle — the seat of impressions, assent, impulse, and moral character."

memes.label: "Spectator"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's theology, the human being is created as a spectator (theatēs) of God and His works, with the capacity to interpret creation."

memes.label: Spiritual Athlete
memes.description: Epictetus's metaphor of the Stoic practitioner as an athlete training in moral virtue, requiring sustained discipline and contest.

memes.label: "Stoicism"
memes.description: "The Hellenistic philosophical school founded by Zeno of Citium, teaching that virtue alone is good and that happiness comes from living in agreement with nature and reason."

memes.label: "Temperance"
memes.description: "One of the four cardinal Stoic virtues — temperance (sōphrosynē) is the power to resist the pull of pleasure and desire, the inner discipline that keeps the ruling faculty from being carried away by attractive appearances."

memes.label: "Three Topics"
memes.description: "The Three Topics (treis topoi) are Epictetus's framework of three fields of philosophical exercise: desire and aversion, impulse and action, and assent and judgment."

memes.label: "Tranquillity"
memes.description: "The untroubled state of mind (ataraxia) that is the product of virtue — freedom from perturbation, achieved by extinguishing desire for things beyond our control."

memes.label: "Trust"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, trust (pistis) is a rational judgment about the reliability of persons and things, closely tied to the faculty of choice and the recognition of what is truly within one's power."

memes.label: "Truth"
memes.description: "In Epictetus's Stoicism, truth (alētheia) is both an epistemic standard — correct judgment about what is and is not in our power — and a moral virtue: the courage to acknowledge reality without evasion, the foundation upon which both caution and courage are built."

memes.label: "Two Handles"
memes.description: "Epictetus's metaphor that 'everything has two handles' — one by which it can be borne and one by which it cannot — teaching the art of framing circumstances."

memes.label: Tyrant
memes.description: The tyrant (tyrannos) as a recurring figure in Stoic ethical training; a test of the philosopher's freedom — one who can chain the body but never the will.

memes.label: "Vice"
memes.description: "The corruption of reason and the sole true evil in Stoic philosophy — a condition of the ruling faculty that mistakes indifferents for goods and evils"

memes.label: "Virtue"
memes.description: "The sole good in Stoic philosophy — moral excellence consisting in living according to reason and nature."

memes.label: "Voyage"
memes.description: "The voyage (plous) as a metaphor for human life in Epictetus — choosing the steersman, coping with storms, and knowing when to disembark."

memes.label: Wealth
memes.description: Material wealth as a classic Stoic indifferent; naturally preferred but neither a moral good nor a source of genuine freedom or mastery.

memes.label: "Wisdom"
memes.description: "The cardinal virtue (sophia) consisting in the knowledge of what is truly good, evil, and indifferent — the knowledge possessed fully only by the Stoic sage."

memes.label: "Wrestler"
memes.description: "The wrestler (palaistēs) as a metaphor for Stoic moral training in Epictetus — God as trainer, the Stoic as athlete matched against a worthy opponent."

memes.label: "Xenophon"
memes.description: "Athenian historian and companion of Socrates (c. 430–354 BCE), cited by Epictetus as the author of the Symposium and a witness to Socrates's power to reconcile quarrels."

memes.label: "Zeno of Citium"
memes.description: "Founder of the Stoic school (c. 334–262 BCE), cited by Epictetus alongside Socrates as a model of the philosopher untroubled by those who hold external power."

memes.label: "Zeus"
memes.description: "The supreme deity in Greek religion — in Stoic theology, identified with the immanent rational principle (*logos*) that orders the cosmos."
